module_description: Students learn the foundations of algebra through an immersive story-based adventure with Arjun and Meena Aunty — moving from unknown values all the way to identifying like and unlike terms.

final_challenge.title: Algebra Detective Challenge
final_challenge.description: Students solve a final mystery using all Module 1 concepts.

screens.title: The Mystery Box
screens.objective: Students discover that unknown values exist and that finding them is satisfying, not scary.

story_segments.speaker: Narrator
story_segments.text: After school on a Tuesday, Arjun ducked into Meena Aunty's sweet shop — the one that always smelled of cardamom and warm ghee. The glass counter was filled with towers of laddoos, barfi, and chakli. But today, something was different.

story_segments.speaker: Meena Aunty
story_segments.text: Arjun! Perfect timing. I have a small puzzle for you. This morning I added 2 laddoos to my jar. Now there are exactly 5 inside. But I forgot how many were there before I added them.

story_segments.speaker: Arjun
story_segments.text: Hmm... so adding 2 gave you 5 total. That means... if I go backwards — 5 minus 2 — there must have been 3 laddoos before!

story_segments.speaker: Meena Aunty
story_segments.text: That's it! You found the hidden number without even opening the jar — just by thinking backwards from what you knew. That hidden starting number? In mathematics, we call it an unknown value.

story_segments.speaker: Arjun
story_segments.text: An unknown value... So it's just a number we don't know yet — but we can figure it out! That doesn't sound scary at all.

story_segments.speaker: Narrator
story_segments.text: An unknown value is a hidden quantity we haven't been told directly. We find it by reversing the operation — working backwards from the result. This is the very first idea in algebra.

concept_explanation.title: Understanding Unknown Values
concept_explanation.main_rule: An unknown value is a hidden quantity. To find it, reverse the operation — work backwards from the result you know.

quiz_questions.question_text: A basket had some mangoes. 4 more were added. Now there are 9. How many were there at the start?
quiz_questions.feedback_correct: Correct! Work backwards: 9 − 4 = 5. You found the unknown!
quiz_questions.feedback_incorrect: Try working backwards from the final total. If adding 4 gave 9, reverse it: 9 minus 4.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of these situations has an unknown value hidden inside it?
options: A box has some coins and 3 more are added to make 10
quiz_questions.correct_answer: A box has some coins and 3 more are added to make 10
quiz_questions.feedback_correct: Exactly right! The starting number of coins is hidden — that's the unknown value.
quiz_questions.feedback_incorrect: Look for a situation where one quantity hasn't been told to you directly. That's the unknown.

quiz_questions.question_text: Meena Aunty's jar had some biscuits. She added 6. Now it has 10. What was the starting number?
quiz_questions.feedback_correct: 10 − 6 = 4. You found the hidden number — just like Arjun did with the laddoos!
quiz_questions.feedback_incorrect: Reverse the addition to find the start. The jar ended with 10, and 6 were added. So how many were there before the 6 came in?

progression.transition_dialogue: Arjun: 'That was fun! But Meena Aunty said something interesting — what if the hidden number could be anything at all, not just one fixed answer? She promised to show me something even smarter tomorrow...'

common_misconceptions: Adding the known number to the total instead of subtracting
Treating the final total as the unknown
Ignoring that the operation needs to be reversed

screens.title: Why Variables Were Invented
screens.objective: Students understand that variables exist because real-life situations have changing quantities, and one letter can represent any number.

story_segments.speaker: Narrator
story_segments.text: The next afternoon, Arjun found Meena Aunty at the counter with a half-finished price board and a frown. She'd been scribbling numbers for an hour — and the board was already out of space.

story_segments.speaker: Meena Aunty
story_segments.text: Each laddoo costs 10 rupees. Simple enough! But every customer buys a different amount. One person wants 2, the next wants 5, someone else wants 12. I've been writing out every possible total and I'm running out of room.

story_segments.speaker: Arjun
story_segments.text: Wait — you're writing a new line for every single customer? That could go on forever! There has to be a smarter way...

story_segments.speaker: Arjun
story_segments.text: What if instead of writing every number separately, we use one letter to stand in for 'however many laddoos the customer buys'? Let's call it n. Then the total is always just: 10 times n. Whatever n happens to be!

story_segments.speaker: Meena Aunty
story_segments.text: Let me check... if n is 1, that's 10. If n is 2, that's 20. If n is 5, that's 50. One rule — 10n — works for every single customer! Arjun, you just invented something mathematicians call a variable!

story_segments.speaker: Narrator
story_segments.text: A variable is a letter that stands for a quantity that can change. Mathematicians invented variables so that one simple rule could work for all situations — instead of writing a different line for each one.

concept_explanation.title: What Are Variables?
concept_explanation.main_rule: A variable is a letter that represents a value that can change. It lets one rule describe many different situations.

examples.meaning: Total cost when each laddoo is ₹10 and n is the number bought
Total cost when each pencil is ₹15 and p is the number bought
Total cost when each item is ₹20 and x is the quantity

quiz_questions.question_text: Each pencil costs 15 rupees. Which expression gives the total cost for any number of pencils?
quiz_questions.feedback_correct: Correct! p stands for however many pencils are bought. 15p works for 1 pencil, 10 pencils, or 100 — every case!
quiz_questions.feedback_incorrect: When the same price applies to a changing quantity, you multiply. 15 times p gives the total for any number.

quiz_questions.question_text: In the expression 20x, what does x represent?
options: A fixed charge added at the end
quiz_questions.feedback_correct: Exactly! x is the variable — it changes depending on how many items the customer buys.
quiz_questions.feedback_incorrect: x is the part that changes — it tracks how many items are involved, not the price of each one.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of these situations actually needs a variable to describe it properly?
options: The cost of buying any number of samosas
quiz_questions.correct_answer: The cost of buying any number of samosas
quiz_questions.feedback_correct: Right! The number of samosas keeps changing per customer — that changing quantity is exactly what a variable is built for.
quiz_questions.feedback_incorrect: Look for a quantity that changes from situation to situation. A fixed amount never needs a variable.

quiz_questions.question_text: Meena Aunty uses the rule 10n. If a customer buys 3 laddoos, what is the total cost?
quiz_questions.feedback_correct: 10 × 3 = 30. You substituted n = 3 into the rule — that's exactly how variables work!
quiz_questions.feedback_incorrect: Replace n with 3 in the expression 10n, then multiply: 10 times 3.

progression.transition_dialogue: Meena Aunty: 'You know what's interesting, Arjun? In 10n, the letter n keeps changing — but the 10 never does. I wonder what we call that steady, unchanging part...'

common_misconceptions: Treating variables as fixed labels rather than changing quantities
Adding the variable and coefficient instead of multiplying
Confusing the variable with the constant multiplier

screens.title: Meet Constants
screens.objective: Students learn that while variables change, some values in an expression are always fixed — these are called constants.

story_segments.speaker: Arjun
story_segments.text: Meena Aunty, I've been thinking about 10n. Every time a new customer walks in, n changes — 1 laddoo, 5 laddoos, 12 laddoos. But the 10... the 10 never moves. It's always 10 rupees per laddoo.

story_segments.speaker: Meena Aunty
story_segments.text: You noticed! The price of one laddoo is fixed. Whether I serve 1 customer or 100, one laddoo always costs 10 rupees. That's never going to change because of who's buying.

story_segments.speaker: Arjun
story_segments.text: So inside an expression, there are two completely different kinds of things? Parts that change — like n — and parts that stay fixed — like 10?

story_segments.speaker: Meena Aunty
story_segments.text: Exactly right. In algebra, the fixed numbers are called constants. Think of them as the reliable, steady parts of an expression — they hold their value no matter what the variable does.

story_segments.speaker: Narrator
story_segments.text: In the expression 10n: n is the variable — it changes with every customer. 10 is the constant — it never changes. Every algebraic expression is built from these two ingredients: things that change, and things that stay fixed.

concept_explanation.title: What Are Constants?
concept_explanation.main_rule: A constant is a fixed number in an expression — it never changes, no matter what the variable becomes.

quiz_questions.question_text: In the expression 8x, which part is the constant?
quiz_questions.feedback_correct: Correct! 8 is always 8 — it never changes. x is the variable that changes. So 8 is the constant.
quiz_questions.feedback_incorrect: The constant is the fixed number. In 8x, the letter x changes depending on the situation. The 8 never does.

quiz_questions.question_text: What makes something a constant in algebra?
options: It is a very large number
It stays fixed and never changes
It is always written as a letter
It changes depending on the situation
quiz_questions.correct_answer: It stays fixed and never changes
quiz_questions.feedback_correct: That's it! Constants are the steady, reliable part of any expression. Their value is set.
quiz_questions.feedback_incorrect: Size or form doesn't make a constant — what matters is that its value never changes.

quiz_questions.question_text: In the expression 6y + 3, how many constants are there?
quiz_questions.feedback_correct: Both 6 and 3 are constants — they are fixed numbers. y is the only variable.
quiz_questions.feedback_incorrect: Count all the fixed numbers: 6 (the coefficient) stays fixed, and 3 (the standalone number) also stays fixed. That's two constants.

progression.transition_dialogue: Arjun: 'So expressions have variables and constants. But I noticed that 3x + 5 looks like it has two separate chunks — one with a letter and one without. What are those chunks called?'

common_misconceptions: Thinking only standalone numbers are constants — coefficients like 6 in 6y are also constants
Confusing the variable (letter) with the constant (number)
Believing a constant can change if the variable changes

screens.title: Meet Terms
screens.objective: Students learn that algebraic expressions are made of individual building blocks called terms — the parts separated by plus or minus signs.

story_segments.speaker: Narrator
story_segments.text: A customer came in with a slightly unusual order. He wanted 3 boxes of laddoos — and since it was a delivery, he'd also have to pay a flat 5 rupee packaging charge. Meena Aunty started writing up the bill.

story_segments.speaker: Meena Aunty
story_segments.text: So we have 3 boxes — let's call x the cost of one box. That gives us 3x for the laddoos. Plus 5 rupees for packaging. The full bill reads: 3x + 5.

story_segments.speaker: Arjun
story_segments.text: Right! The bill has two separate parts. 3x is the laddoo cost — it changes depending on the box price. And 5 is the packaging fee — it's always the same. They're joined by the plus sign, but the plus sign itself isn't a charge. It's just connecting the two actual parts.

story_segments.speaker: Meena Aunty
story_segments.text: That's a wonderful way to see it. In algebra, each of those meaningful pieces — 3x and 5 — has its own name. They're called terms. The plus or minus signs between them are just separators, not terms themselves.

story_segments.speaker: Narrator
story_segments.text: Terms are the individual parts of an algebraic expression. They are separated by plus (+) or minus (−) signs. The signs are separators — they sit between terms, but they are not terms themselves. In 3x + 5, there are exactly two terms: 3x and 5.

concept_explanation.title: What Are Terms?
concept_explanation.main_rule: Terms are the individual parts of an expression separated by addition or subtraction signs. The signs themselves are not terms.

quiz_questions.question_text: How many terms are in the expression 3x + 5?
quiz_questions.feedback_correct: Correct! 3x is one term and 5 is another. The plus sign just connects them — it isn't a term.
quiz_questions.feedback_incorrect: Count the meaningful parts separated by the plus sign. The plus sign itself is not a term.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of these is NOT a term in an algebraic expression?
quiz_questions.feedback_correct: Right! The plus sign is a separator — it lives between terms but is not one itself.
quiz_questions.feedback_incorrect: 3x, 5, and y are all meaningful algebraic parts — they are all terms. The + sign just separates them.

quiz_questions.question_text: How many terms does the expression 4a + 2b + 7 have?
quiz_questions.feedback_correct: Exactly! 4a is one term, 2b is a second term, and 7 is a third term. Two plus signs, three terms.
quiz_questions.feedback_incorrect: Separate the expression at each plus sign: 4a | 2b | 7. Count the pieces, not the signs.

quiz_questions.question_text: In the expression x + y + 8, which is the complete list of all the terms?
quiz_questions.feedback_correct: All three are terms — x, y, and 8. The two plus signs between them are just separators, not terms.
quiz_questions.feedback_incorrect: Every meaningful piece counts: x, y, and 8 are all terms. Only the plus signs are excluded.

progression.transition_dialogue: Arjun: 'So we have terms like 3x, 5x, and 2y. Some of these look really similar — they both have x! I wonder if similar-looking terms can be treated the same way... like counted together?'

common_misconceptions: Counting the plus or minus operator as a term
Missing standalone constants like 7 in 4a + 2b + 7 — they are terms too
Thinking only variable-containing parts are terms

screens.title: Like Terms and Unlike Terms
screens.objective: Students learn to recognise which terms share the same variable (like terms) and which do not (unlike terms) — the gateway to simplification.

story_segments.speaker: Narrator
story_segments.text: End of the day. Meena Aunty was counting her remaining stock. On the counter she laid out three piles: laddoos she'd kept aside in the morning, more laddoos from the afternoon, and a separate pile of barfi she hadn't sold.

story_segments.speaker: Meena Aunty
story_segments.text: I have 3 laddoos here, 5 laddoos over there, and 2 barfi on the side. Arjun, I want one total count for each type. Can I just add all of them up into a single number?

story_segments.speaker: Arjun
story_segments.text: Of course not! Laddoos and barfi are completely different things. You can add 3 laddoos and 5 laddoos — that's 8 laddoos. But the 2 barfi stay separate because they're a different type entirely. You'd never call 8 laddoos plus 2 barfi 'ten sweets of the same kind.'

story_segments.speaker: Meena Aunty
story_segments.text: Now write it the algebra way. Let x stand for laddoos and y stand for barfi. We had 3x, 5x, and 2y. The 3x and 5x can be grouped — they're the same type. The 2y stands alone.

story_segments.speaker: Arjun
story_segments.text: So 3x and 5x are like terms because they both have the variable x — just like both piles were laddoos. But 2y is an unlike term because y is a completely different variable. Different types, different rules!

story_segments.speaker: Narrator
story_segments.text: Like terms share exactly the same variable. Unlike terms have different variables. Like terms can eventually be combined — unlike terms cannot. The coefficient (the number in front) doesn't matter for deciding: 3x and 7x are like terms. 3x and 3y are unlike terms.

concept_explanation.title: Like Terms and Unlike Terms
concept_explanation.main_rule: Like terms have the same variable. Unlike terms have different variables. The number in front (coefficient) does not determine whether terms are like or unlike.

examples.reason: x and y are different variables
Same coefficient, but a and b are different variables

quiz_questions.question_text: Which pair of terms are like terms?
quiz_questions.feedback_correct: Correct! Both 3x and 5x have the variable x — same type, so they're like terms.
quiz_questions.feedback_incorrect: Like terms must share the same variable. Look for the pair where both terms use identical letters.

quiz_questions.question_text: Why are 4y and 7y considered like terms?
options: Because they have the same number in front
Because they share the same variable
Because they are both written with two characters
Because their numbers add up to 11
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Because they share the same variable
quiz_questions.feedback_correct: That's it! The variable — y — is what makes them the same type. The coefficients 4 and 7 don't matter for this decision.
quiz_questions.feedback_incorrect: Coefficients are not what defines like terms. It's the variable that counts — both have y.

options: Yes, because they both have 5 in front
No, because their variables are different
Yes, because both coefficients are identical
quiz_questions.correct_answer: No, because their variables are different
quiz_questions.feedback_correct: Correct! a and b are different variables — just like laddoos and barfi are different sweets. Same coefficient doesn't make them like terms.
quiz_questions.feedback_incorrect: The 5 in front doesn't determine anything here. a and b are completely different variables — so 5a and 5b are unlike terms.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of these is a like term for 3x?
quiz_questions.feedback_correct: 7x is correct! It has the variable x, just like 3x. The fact that the coefficient is different (7 instead of 3) doesn't matter — same variable means like terms.
quiz_questions.feedback_incorrect: Focus on the variable: 3x has x. Find the option that also has x — the number in front doesn't matter.

progression.transition_dialogue: Arjun: 'So like terms can go together — 3x and 5x are the same type. But how do we actually combine them? That must involve adding somehow...' | Meena Aunty: 'That's exactly where we're headed next. Module 2 — Simplification. You're ready, Arjun.'

common_misconceptions: Grouping terms by matching coefficients instead of matching variables
Thinking 5a and 5b are like terms because both have 5
Combining unlike terms together — a classic and very common error that Module 2 addresses directly
